source.sourcePath: raw/sources/RA/05_해외 등록 서류 (영업팀 F-up)/260520 우크라이나(Licarno) 23G, P1 - 이창민 팀장님/260602 추가 제출본/QMS/AQP705 Usability Evaluation Regulation/AQP705_Usability Evaluation Regulation.pdf

sourceSummary.oneLine: Internal QMS regulation AQP705 defines the usability engineering process for company-manufactured medical devices.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Used to control usability evaluation activities during design and development, including user specifications, risk-related use scenarios, formative evaluation, summative evaluation, UOUP handling, and usability engineering records.
keyPoints: Purpose is to develop user-centered medical devices, reduce use errors and associated risks, and enhance safety and effectiveness.
Applies to overall design and development activities for products manufactured by the company and to basic QMS requirements.
Revision 1 changed the document number from AQP718 to AQP705 and revised the procedure to comply with KGMP, EN ISO 13485:2016+A11:2021, FDA 21 CFR Part 820, and MDR (EU) 2017/745 Article 10.
Development Team is responsible for usability assessments, risk analysis and management, user interface analysis, and post-production information analysis.
Usability Engineering Plan and Usability Engineering Report are controlled forms retained by the Development Team for 10 years.

concepts.name: Usability engineering
concepts.evidence: Applying knowledge of human behavior, abilities, limitations, and other characteristics to the design of medical devices (including software), systems, and tasks to achieve appropriate usability.

concepts.name: Usability engineering file
concepts.evidence: A collection of records and related documents produced in the usability engineering process.

concepts.name: Formative evaluation
concepts.evidence: A user interface evaluation deliberately conducted to identify strengths, weaknesses, and unexpected usability errors in the user interface design.

concepts.name: Summative evaluation
concepts.evidence: Evaluation of the user interface, intentionally performed at the end of user interface development, to obtain objective evidence that the user interface can be used safely.

concepts.name: User interface of unknown provenance
concepts.evidence: User interfaces or portions of user interfaces of previously developed medical devices for which there is no sufficient documentation of the usability engineering process.

concepts.name: Use error
concepts.evidence: User actions or omitted user actions while using a medical device that cause results different from the manufacturer's intention or the user's expectations.

concepts.name: Use specification
concepts.evidence: Summary of important characteristics relevant to the context of use of medical devices

concepts.name: User interface specification
concepts.evidence: A collection of specifications that comprehensively and predictably describe the user interface of medical devices.

concepts.name: Task analysis
concepts.evidence: Analytics used to determine user goals and specific actions that must be performed when a user operates a medical device or performs a task.

evidence.claim: The document is a controlled Usability Evaluation Regulation.
evidence.evidenceText: 사용적합성 평가 규정 (Usability Evaluation Regulation) Distinguishment ■ Controlled □ Uncontrolled

evidence.claim: The document number is AQP705.
evidence.evidenceText: Usability Evaluation Regulation Document No AQP705

evidence.claim: The revision number is 1 and revision date is 2024.04.01.
evidence.evidenceText: Revision date 2024.04.01. Rev. No 1

evidence.claim: Revision 1 changed the document number from AQP718 to AQP705.
evidence.evidenceText: 1) Document number change: AQP718 → AQP705

evidence.claim: Revision 1 was made to comply with KGMP, EN ISO 13485:2016+A11:2021, FDA 21 CFR Part 820, and MDR (EU) 2017/745 Article 10 requirements.
evidence.evidenceText: Comprehensive revision to comply with KGMP, EN ISO 13485:2016+A11:2021, FDA 21 CFR Part 820, and MDR (EU) 2017/745 Article 10 requirements

evidence.claim: The purpose is to apply usability engineering with design and development activities to reduce use errors and associated risks and enhance medical device safety and effectiveness.
evidence.evidenceText: Through usability engineering, it aims to develop user-centered medical devices, reduce use errors and associated risks, and enhance the safety and effectiveness of medical devices.

evidence.claim: The regulation applies to overall design and development activities for products manufactured by the company.
evidence.evidenceText: This regulation applies to the overall activities related to the design and development of products manufactured by the company, including planning, inputs and outputs, review, verification, validation, drawing control, changes, and transfer

evidence.claim: The CEO is responsible for providing resources, approving usability assessment activity results, and final approval of the usability report.
evidence.evidenceText: 5.1 CEO 1) Ensure provision of human and material resources necessary for usability assessments. 2) Approval of the results of usability assessment activities. 3) Final approval of the usability report.

evidence.claim: The Quality Manager oversees usability assessment activities and reviews usability assessment plans and reports.
evidence.evidenceText: 5.2 Quality Manager 1) Oversee usability assessment activities. 2) Review and approve usability assessment-related actions. 3) Review usability assessment plans and reports.

evidence.claim: The Development Team concurrently serves as Risk Manager and conducts risk analysis, risk management, usability assessments, and post-production information analysis.
evidence.evidenceText: 5.3 Development Team (※ Concurrently serves as Risk Manager) ... Conduct risk analysis and management activities. ... Analyze post-production information (feedback, customer complaints, adverse events, etc.) and determine corrective actions. ... Conduct usability assessments and perform overall tasks.

evidence.claim: User specifications must include intended indication, patient population, body part or tissue, user profile, use environment, and principle of operation.
evidence.evidenceText: The development team prepares a user specification that includes the following. 1) Intended medical indication ... 2) Intended patient population ... 3) Type of body part or tissue ... 4) Intended user profile ... 5) Intended use environment ... 6) Principle of operation

evidence.claim: Safety-related user interface features are identified as part of risk analysis in accordance with ISO 14971:2019, 5.3.
evidence.evidenceText: The development team shall identify user interface features that may be safety-relevant as part of the risk analysis performed in accordance with ISO 14971:2019, 5.3.

evidence.claim: Known or foreseeable hazards and hazardous situations are identified as part of ISO 14971:2019, 5.4 risk analysis.
evidence.evidenceText: This identification should be performed as part of the risk analysis (ISO 14971:2019, 5.4) during medical device risk management.

evidence.claim: Hazard-related use scenarios may be selected for overall evaluation based on severity or risk of hazards that may arise from use errors.
evidence.evidenceText: Usage scenarios related to hazard factors can be selected based on the severity or risk of hazards that may arise from usage errors (see ISO 14971:2019, 5.5)

evidence.claim: User interface specifications must include user requirements, technical user interface requirements, supporting documentation requirements, and separate training requirements.
evidence.evidenceText: The user interface specification must include. 1) User requirements 2) Technical requirements related to the user interface (display color, text size, etc.) 3) Requirement of supporting documentation 4) Requirement of separate training

evidence.claim: Risk control for use errors follows priority order: design changes, protective measures, and safety information.
evidence.evidenceText: control risks related to usage errors by using one or more of the following items listed in order of priority. 1) Design changes to medical devices 2) Establishment of protective measures ... 3) Providing information on safety

evidence.claim: A user interface evaluation plan must document purpose and planned formative and summative evaluation methods.
evidence.evidenceText: The user interface evaluation plan should. ① Document the purpose and identify planned formative and summative evaluation methods

evidence.claim: Formative evaluations must be conducted at least 2-3 times.
evidence.evidenceText: The development team must conduct formative evaluations at least 2-3 times to identify shortcomings in the user interface and improve them through the risk control methods in Section 5.6.

evidence.claim: Summative evaluation checks whether use-related risks have been appropriately controlled and whether the user interface can be used effectively and safely.
evidence.evidenceText: determine whether use-related risks have been appropriately controlled and whether the user interface can be used effectively and safely.

evidence.claim: If new use errors, hazards, hazardous situations, or hazard-related use scenarios are discovered during summative data analysis, the regulation activities should be repeated as appropriate.
evidence.evidenceText: If new usage errors, hazards, hazardous situations, or usage scenarios related to hazards are discovered during this data analysis, the activities outlined in this regulation should be repeated as appropriate.

evidence.claim: For UOUP without adequate IEC 62366-1:2015 usability engineering process records, a specific UOUP process applies.
evidence.evidenceText: For user interfaces or user interface parts that do not have adequate development records using the usability engineering process of IEC 62366-1:2015, the process of 5.10.2 applies.

evidence.claim: Usability Engineering Plan form 705-1 is managed by the Development Team and retained for 10 years.
evidence.evidenceText: 1 Usability Engineering Plan 705-1 Development Team 10 years

evidence.claim: Usability Engineering Report form 705-2 is managed by the Development Team and retained for 10 years.
evidence.evidenceText: 2 Usability Engineering Report 705-2 Development Team 10 years

reviewFlags.reason: No product model is identified in the source text; productSignal is Unclassified.
AuthoritySignal is FDA, but the document also cites KGMP, EN ISO 13485:2016+A11:2021, MDR (EU) 2017/745 Article 10, ISO 14971:2019, ISO 24971:2020, and IEC 62366-1:2015.
Text quality contains OCR or translation artifacts, including 'This document is effective from the date of appro.', 'Distinguishment', and inconsistent spacing in page headers.
The procedure section is numbered 6.x, but multiple internal references cite Section 5.x or Article 5.1 through 5.10, creating ambiguous cross-references.
